## 1. Executive Summary

Universe Simulation is a multiplayer 3D browser game set in a procedurally generated open world. Players sign in, spawn into a shared landscape, and navigate with mouse and keyboard through a first-person-style environment. The world has a simulated clock that runs continuously on a dedicated server even when no players are online. That clock advances through real cosmological epochs — from the Planck era through nucleosynthesis, the dark ages, reionization, and into the stellar age — letting the world age over time independent of any individual player.

As of this report the game has moved substantially forward on two fronts: infrastructure stability (all server errors resolved in prior sessions) and core simulation depth. The most recent prior session delivered Simulation Grid Phase 1 — a complete, real chemistry-based fire simulation. For the first time, fire in the game is not a scripted visual effect. It is an Arrhenius combustion reaction running inside a 3D grid of cells, consuming oxygen, spreading heat, and burning out when fuel is exhausted — all calculated using real thermodynamic formulas.

This session adds a separate, publicly accessible Status Website — a live Mission Control dashboard available at /status/ on the deployed site. Anyone with the URL can open this page and watch the game world in real time without being logged in or playing the game. The dashboard shows player positions on a procedurally rendered satellite map, NPC behavior distribution, server connection status, current cosmological epoch, and simulation time — all streamed live from the Railway WebSocket server at 10 Hz.

The game continues to have known gaps — NPC interaction, terrain height variation, and six placeholder panels — but the core resource and chemistry loop is genuinely functional and scientifically grounded, and observers now have a dedicated window into the world's live state.

---

## 2. Application Overview

**What does this app do?**
It is a multiplayer 3D survival and progression game played entirely inside a web browser. Players explore a shared world, manage survival stats (health, hunger, thirst, energy), gather resources, craft items, unlock technologies, and evolve their character over a cosmological timeline. The world clock runs continuously at a server level, meaning the universe "ages" in real time even when you are offline.

**Who is it for?**
The target audience appears to be players interested in open-world survival games with a science-fiction or cosmological theme. The cosmological epoch system — where the in-game clock maps to real astrophysics milestones — suggests an audience comfortable with abstract or speculative themes.

**What problem does it solve?**
It provides a persistent shared world experience accessible without downloading any software. Because everything runs in the browser, the barrier to entry is low.

**Technology stack (explained simply)**

| Technology | Plain-English Role |
|---|---|
| React | The framework that builds and updates the on-screen interface |
| Three.js / React Three Fiber | The 3D rendering engine that draws the world, player, and NPCs |
| Vite | The tool that bundles and builds the code for the browser |
| TypeScript | A version of JavaScript with strict type checking, which catches mistakes before the game runs |
| Zustand | A lightweight system for storing and sharing game state (player health, position, etc.) |
| Clerk | Handles user login and authentication |
| Neon Postgres | A cloud database that stores player save data and world settings |
| Vercel | Hosts the frontend website and the save/load API endpoints |
| Railway | Hosts the always-on WebSocket server that manages the live multiplayer world |
| WebSocket | A persistent two-way connection between each browser and the Railway server, used for real-time multiplayer |
| Rapier3D | A physics engine (WebAssembly-based) for collision detection and character movement |
| bitecs | An entity-component system library used to manage game entities (player metabolism, position, etc.) |
| Web Workers | Background threads running chemistry and physics calculations independently of the render loop |
| SharedArrayBuffer | Shared memory between workers and the main thread for lock-free grid state |

---

## 3. Project Structure

The repository is organized into clearly separated concerns. Here is what each major folder contains:

```
universe-sim/
  api/              Vercel serverless functions (the HTTP endpoints)
    save.ts         Saves a player's state to the database
    load.ts         Loads a player's state from the database
    world-settings.ts  Gets/sets the world time scale (admin only for writes)
    admin.ts        Returns all player records (admin only)
    init-db.ts      Creates the database tables on first run
  server/           The Railway WebSocket server (always-on Node.js process)
    src/
      index.js           Entry point, WebSocket message handling
      WorldClock.js      The authoritative simulation clock
      NpcManager.js      Manages 50 server-side NPCs
      PlayerRegistry.js  Tracks connected players
      BroadcastScheduler.js  Sends world snapshots to all clients at 10 Hz
      WorldSettingsSync.js   Reads/writes time scale and sim time to the database
  src/              Frontend source code
    App.tsx         Root component: login screen or game
    main.tsx        Application entry point
    net/            WebSocket client code
    store/          Game state stores (player, game, multiplayer, UI, save)
    rendering/      3D scene, player mesh, terrain, NPC rendering, FireRenderer
    ui/             HUD, panels, admin panel, notifications
    player/         Player controller, inventory, crafting recipes
    ecs/            Entity-Component System for metabolism and health
    engine/         Simulation engine wrapper + LocalSimManager
    sim/            Chemistry and thermal simulation grid
      MaterialRegistry.ts    11-material thermal constants table
      GridCoords.ts          World-to-grid coordinate math
      Arrhenius.ts           Combustion rate equations
    workers/        Web workers
      chemWorker.ts          Arrhenius combustion at 10 Hz
      thermalWorker.ts       Heat diffusion (now per-material)
    status/         Status website source code (NEW)
      main.tsx               Status page entry point
      StatusApp.tsx          Root layout and data orchestration
      hooks/
        useStatusSocket.ts   WebSocket connection and data types
      components/
        EpochBar.tsx         Top header strip
        SatelliteMap.tsx     Live 2D map canvas
        ServerStats.tsx      Right-side stats sidebar
        PlayerRoster.tsx     Bottom player card strip
        PlayerDetail.tsx     Click-to-expand player modal
  status/           Status page HTML entry point (NEW)
    index.html      Vite entry point for the /status/ route
  vercel.json       Vercel configuration (sets required browser headers)
  vite.config.ts    Build configuration — defines two entry points (main + status)
  package.json      Node.js dependency manifest
```

**Key observation**: The Vite build now has two named entry points — `main` (the game, at `index.html`) and `status` (the dashboard, at `status/index.html`). Both are compiled as part of the same build process and deployed together to Vercel. The status site is fully standalone and shares no game-specific code — it imports only from its own `src/status/` subtree.

---

## 4. Features and Functionality

### Working Features

**User Authentication**
Players sign in with Clerk before entering the game. The login screen displays the game title against a dark space background. On first load, the app checks authentication status and either shows the login form or enters the game immediately.

**3D World Navigation**
- The player spawns in a 512x512 unit flat green terrain at coordinates (0, 0.9, 0)
- Movement: WASD keys to walk, mouse to look around
- A click-to-play overlay is shown on the game canvas — clicking it locks the mouse cursor to the screen for first-person-style control
- Pressing Escape or opening any panel releases the mouse cursor
- The camera is positioned behind and above the player for a third-person perspective
- A sky with a visible sun position and a star field are rendered
- Directional light with shadow casting is active

**Player Character**
The player is represented as a blue capsule body with a skin-toned sphere head and two small black eyes indicating facing direction.

**Vitals System**
Five survival statistics are tracked in real time and displayed as colored progress bars in the top-left corner of the screen:
- Health (red) — decreases if other vitals are critical
- Satiety (orange) — decreases over time (hunger building) — empties in approximately 20 minutes
- Hydration (blue) — decreases over time (thirst building) — empties in approximately 12 minutes
- Energy (green) — fluctuates with activity and rest
- Stamina (purple) — depletes with exertion

All five bars turn red and flash as a warning when they fall below 25%.

**Evolution Points**
A gold "EP" counter below the vitals bars tracks accumulated evolution points. These feed into the evolution panel.

**Current Goal Badge**
A small blue badge below the vitals shows the player's current high-level objective (e.g., "SURVIVE").

**HUD Clock and Connection Status**
Top-right corner shows:
- The current cosmological epoch name (e.g., "STELLAR")
- The simulation time in human-readable format (seconds, minutes, hours, days, years, kyr, Myr, Gyr)
- A colored dot indicating multiplayer connection status (green = connected, yellow = connecting, red = offline)
- A player count when connected
- A "PAUSED" indicator when an admin has paused the world

**HUD Ambient Temperature Display**
The HUD also displays the ambient temperature near the player, color-coded:
- Blue: below 0°C
- Green: 0–30°C (normal outdoor)
- Orange: 30–50°C (hot)
- Red: 50°C and above (dangerous)

The number updates in real time as the player moves toward or away from heat sources.

**Multiplayer**
- Players see each other as colored capsule shapes in real time
- Position updates are sent to the server at 10 times per second
- The server broadcasts a world snapshot (all player positions, NPC positions, clock state) to all clients at 10 Hz
- Automatic reconnection with exponential backoff (doubles wait time up to 30 seconds after disconnection)

**NPC Presence**
50 server-managed NPCs appear as orange spheres. They wander randomly within a 200-unit radius, occasionally changing direction. Their positions are synchronized from the server.

**Gathering and Inventory**
Players press F near resource nodes (trees, flint deposits) to collect materials directly into their inventory. The inventory holds 40 slots. Items display name, quantity, and quality.

**Equip System**
Players can open inventory (I), click an item, and click Equip. A gray box appears near the player's right hand showing the equipped item. The equipped item determines what harvesting and interactions are available.

**Crafting**
The crafting panel shows available recipes filtered by materials in the player's inventory and current civilization tier. Recipes show ingredient requirements in green (available) or red (missing). Crafting produces toast notifications on success or failure.

**Fire Starting — Real Chemistry**
This is the first end-to-end chemistry interaction in the game:
1. Gather wood (F near tree node)
2. Gather flint (F near flint deposit)
3. Equip flint from inventory
4. Left-click near a wood or bark resource node
5. "Fire started!" message appears
6. Real combustion begins in the chemistry grid

**Simulation Grid — Chemistry Worker**
A background worker runs at 10 Hz calculating:
- Arrhenius combustion rates for wood, bark, fiber, and coal cells
- Oxygen consumption in burning cells
- Cell burnout — when density drops below 1 kg/m³, the cell converts to air (ash)
- Energy deposit (IGNITE message) and material placement (PLACE_MATERIAL message)
- Per-material thermal properties instead of the previous hardcoded water constants

**Fire Renderer**
A React Three Fiber component reads the chemistry grid 10 times per second and draws visible fire effects for any cell above 200°C:
- A glowing sphere whose size scales with temperature
- A point light that illuminates nearby terrain and objects in real time

**Physics**
The Rapier3D WebAssembly physics engine is active. The terrain is a trimesh collider. The player has a capsule-shaped rigid body and KinematicCharacterController. Gravity points toward the planet center.

**Status Website — Mission Control Dashboard (NEW)**
A separate public-facing page accessible at /status/ shows the live state of the game world without requiring a login. Full details in Section 14.

**Sidebar Panels (8 panels)**
A 48-pixel-wide icon strip on the right edge of the screen provides access to eight panels. Each has a keyboard shortcut:

| Panel | Key | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Inventory | I | Working (displays 40 slots, shows item details, can drop items) |
| Crafting | C | Working (filters by available materials and civ tier) |
| Tech Tree | T | Panel opens, content is a placeholder |
| Evolution | E | Panel opens, content is a placeholder |
| Journal | J | Panel opens, content is a placeholder |
| Character | Tab | Panel opens, content is a placeholder |
| Map | M | Panel opens, content is a placeholder |
| Settings | Esc | Panel opens, content is a placeholder |

When any panel is open: the mouse cursor is released from pointer lock, game movement input is blocked, and the panel slides in from the right with a smooth animation.

**Save and Load**
- Player state is loaded from the Neon database immediately after login
- Auto-save runs every 60 seconds
- Save also runs when the player closes the tab or logs out
- Saved fields include: position, all five vitals, evolution points, civilization tier, discovered items, current goal, and cumulative sim time

**Admin Controls**
For the designated administrator account only:
- A red "ADMIN" button appears in the bottom-right corner
- Opens a full-screen overlay showing a table of all registered players with their vitals, position, sim time, civ tier, and goal
- "Focus" button on each row moves the admin's camera to spectate that player's location
- "Free Camera" button returns to normal play
- Time controls (pause/resume, and time scale from 0.1x to 1,000,000x) are available via the TimeControls component
- Changes to time scale are broadcast to all connected players via the Railway WebSocket server and also persisted to the database

**Simulation Clock**
The cosmological epochs mapped to the game's timeline (based on real astrophysics):

| Epoch | Triggered at |
|---|---|
| Planck | less than 0.1 nanoseconds of sim time |
| Grand Unification | less than 1 microsecond |
| Electroweak | less than 0.1 milliseconds |
| Quark Epoch | less than 10 milliseconds |
| Nucleosynthesis | less than 1 second |
| Photon Epoch | less than 380,000 years |
| Dark Ages | less than 100 million years |
| Reionization | less than 1 billion years |
| Stellar | less than 4 billion years |
| Galactic | less than 10 billion years |
| Contemporary | less than 14 billion years |
| Stellar Late | less than 100 trillion years |
| Degenerate | less than 10^40 years |
| Dark Era | Beyond |

### Features That Exist but Are Incomplete

**Tech Tree, Evolution, Journal, Character, Map, Settings**: Panels open and close correctly. Their content areas are currently empty placeholders.

---

## 5. User Interface and Design

**Visual Theme**
The UI uses a consistent dark space aesthetic: near-black backgrounds, semi-transparent frosted panels (blur effect), white monospace typography, and blue accent colors. Warning states use red. The palette is cohesive and appropriate for the genre.

**Layout**
- Top-left: Vitals panel (fixed, always visible during play) — includes ambient temperature reading
- Top-right: Clock and connection status (fixed, offset to avoid icon strip)
- Right edge: 48px icon strip with 8 panel buttons
- Full viewport: 3D canvas (fills entire screen behind the UI)
- Center: Crosshair when pointer is locked; click-to-play overlay when it is not
- Bottom-right: Admin button (admin accounts only)

**Navigation**
All navigation is keyboard-driven during gameplay. Panels are toggled by single-key shortcuts. Escape is contextual — it closes an open panel first; if no panel is open, it opens Settings.

**Responsiveness**
The game is designed for desktop browsers. It relies on pointer lock (mouse look), keyboard WASD input, and a wide panel layout (480px). It is not usable on touch devices or narrow screens.

**Panel Animation**
Panels slide in from the right with a smooth easing curve over 250 milliseconds. The transition does not flash or jump.

**Notifications**
A toast notification system exists and is wired to the UI store. Crafting events, harvest events ("Fire started!"), and warnings trigger notifications. Save failure notifications are not yet implemented.

---

## 6. Performance Analysis

**Rendering**
- Three.js canvas fills the entire viewport with antialiasing and high-performance GPU preference enabled
- Shadow maps are rendered at 2048x2048 pixels — a high-quality setting that is GPU-intensive
- The terrain is a single flat plane mesh (512x512 units, 63x63 subdivisions — 3,969 quads)
- The sky uses a procedural shader
- 5,000 stars are rendered via the Stars component
- Remote players and NPCs are simple geometric shapes (capsules and spheres), which is efficient
- Fire cells are rendered as glowing spheres with point lights — each burning cell adds one draw call and one dynamic light
- The game loop runs at the browser's frame rate (typically 60 fps) via requestAnimationFrame

**Simulation Grid**
- The chemistry worker runs at 10 Hz in a separate thread — it does not block rendering
- The fire renderer polls the grid at 10 Hz (every 100ms) inside a useFrame callback with a delta accumulator
- Grid size is bounded to the player's local area — it does not simulate the entire world simultaneously

**Network**
- Player position updates: 10 per second per client
- Server-to-client world snapshots: 10 per second
- Auto-reconnect with exponential backoff prevents reconnection storms

**Potential Performance Concerns**
- Shadow map size of 2048x2048 with a large shadow camera frustum (512x512 units) may cause GPU stalls on integrated graphics or older hardware
- No level-of-detail system exists — as NPC count or player count increases, all entities render at full detail regardless of distance
- Large fires with many burning cells will add proportionally more point lights — this is physically correct but may degrade GPU performance if many cells burn simultaneously. A maximum point light budget may be needed in future.

---

## 7. Accessibility Review

The application has limited accessibility support, which is expected for a mouse-and-keyboard 3D game. Specific observations:

**What works**
- Sidebar icon buttons include aria-label attributes so screen readers can identify them
- Icon buttons use aria-pressed to indicate active state
- The close button on panels has aria-label="Close panel"
- Time control buttons include aria-label and aria-pressed attributes
- Color changes to vital bars are accompanied by percentage text labels, so they are not color-only indicators
- Temperature display uses both color AND a numeric value, so it is not color-only

**What is missing or problematic**
- The game canvas has no keyboard-only play mode — it requires pointer lock, which is mouse-dependent
- No focus management when panels open — keyboard focus does not move into the panel automatically
- Panel content has no focus trap, meaning the Tab key will cycle through the entire page while a panel is open
- Color contrast in secondary text (for example the gray-on-dark text used in labels) may fail WCAG AA contrast requirements
- No reduced-motion support — the panel slide animation will play regardless of the user's system accessibility settings

**WCAG Compliance**: Approximately Level A for the UI elements that have been labeled. The core 3D gameplay is not accessible by design given the medium.

---

## 8. Security Assessment

**Authentication**
Clerk handles all user authentication. The Vercel API routes verify the Clerk JWT (a cryptographically signed proof of identity) on every request using the CLERK_SECRET_KEY environment variable. This is a correct and secure pattern.

**Authorization**
- The /api/save and /api/load endpoints check that the requesting user's identity matches the data being accessed — a player can only save or load their own data
- The /api/world-settings POST and /api/admin endpoints check that the user ID matches ADMIN_USER_ID before allowing access
- The admin check in AdminPanel.tsx uses VITE_ADMIN_USER_ID on the client side to hide the admin button — this is a UI convenience only, not a security boundary. The actual security check happens server-side in the API routes, which is correct

**Identified Risks**

| Risk | Severity | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| ADMIN_SET_TIME WebSocket command has no authentication | Medium | Any client that connects to the Railway WebSocket can send ADMIN_SET_TIME and change the world clock for all players. The server does not verify whether the sender is actually the admin. |
| admin.ts still uses Web API style (Request/Response) | Medium | This endpoint was not converted during the fix session. It will return 500 errors on Vercel's Node.js runtime. |
| Status site connects to WebSocket as an observer with no authentication | Low | The status page joins the game server using a hardcoded observer identity. The server broadcasts to all connected sockets, so the observer receives the same WORLD_SNAPSHOT as any player. No sensitive data beyond position and health is exposed, but operators should be aware the feed is unauthenticated. |
| No rate limiting on save/load endpoints | Low | A malicious user could spam /api/save with rapid requests. Vercel's default limits provide some protection but no explicit rate limit is configured. |
| discoveries field is stored as a raw JSON string | Low | The field is written with JSON.stringify and read with JSON.parse rather than using a native database JSON column type. This works but is fragile if the database is queried directly. |
| /api/init-db has no authentication | Low | Anyone who knows the URL can call this endpoint. It is safe to call repeatedly (it uses CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS) but should be removed or protected after initial setup. |

---

## 9. Code Quality

**Overall Assessment**: The code is well-organized and readable. The separation between frontend, API layer, and game server is clean. The new simulation modules (`src/sim/`) are pure functions with no side effects, making them easy to test and reason about. 18 unit tests were added in the prior session — the first test coverage in the project.

**Strengths**
- TypeScript is used throughout the frontend and API layer, providing type safety
- Zustand stores are small and focused — each manages a single domain (player, game, multiplayer, UI)
- The WorldSocket class correctly handles reconnection logic and avoids memory leaks by canceling timers on destroy
- The server's WorldClock uses wall-clock delta time rather than a fixed interval counter, which prevents time drift when the process is briefly delayed
- Comments throughout the code explain the reasoning behind decisions, not just what the code does
- The entity-component system correctly separates data (components) from behavior (systems)
- The save operation uses SQL upsert (INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE), which is the correct pattern for "save or overwrite"
- MaterialRegistry, GridCoords, and Arrhenius are pure functions with zero side effects — easy to test and reuse
- All three new simulation modules use real physical constants from the existing constants.ts file, maintaining consistency
- The chemistry worker uses a message-passing protocol (IGNITE, PLACE_MATERIAL) that is clean and extensible
- The status site is cleanly isolated in its own `src/status/` subtree — it shares no code with the game and has no dependencies on game stores, authentication, or 3D libraries
- `useStatusSocket` filters out the observer's own JOIN entry from the player list, preventing the dashboard from accidentally showing "~observer" as a player

**Weaknesses**
- admin.ts uses the old Web API Request/Response style while the other three routes were updated to Node.js req/res style — an inconsistency that causes real failures
- Six of eight panels are empty placeholders with no implementation
- The InventoryPanel forces a re-render using a useState counter (forceRefresh) as a manual refresh trigger — a sign that the inventory singleton does not use reactive state
- The saveGame function does not handle errors or notify the user when a save fails
- Test coverage exists only for the three new simulation modules — the rest of the codebase remains untested

**Testing**
- Vitest is configured as the test runner
- 18 tests exist covering MaterialRegistry (4 tests), GridCoords (8 tests), and Arrhenius (6 tests)
- All 18 tests pass
- No test coverage for game logic, API routes, WebSocket behavior, inventory, crafting, player systems, or the status site components

---

**Note on duplicate Clerk packages**: Both @clerk/clerk-react (version 5) and @clerk/react (version 6) are listed as dependencies. These are the same package. The codebase imports from @clerk/react in most files. The older @clerk/clerk-react entry should be removed from package.json to avoid version conflicts and bundle bloat.

**Note on status site dependencies**: The status website uses only React and the browser's native WebSocket API. It does not bring in any additional dependencies. Fonts (IBM Plex Mono and Orbitron) are loaded from Google Fonts via a link tag in status/index.html.

---

## 11. APIs and Integrations

### Vercel API Routes (HTTP)

These run as serverless functions — they start on demand when a request arrives and shut down after responding.

| Endpoint | Method | Auth Required | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| /api/save | POST | Yes (any signed-in user) | Upserts player state to player_saves table |
| /api/load | GET | Yes (any signed-in user) | Retrieves player state from player_saves table |
| /api/world-settings | GET | No | Returns current time_scale from world_settings table |
| /api/world-settings | POST | Yes (admin only) | Updates time_scale in world_settings table |
| /api/admin | GET | Yes (admin only) | Returns all player records ordered by last update |
| /api/init-db | GET | No | Creates database tables — intended for one-time setup only |

### Railway WebSocket Server (Real-time)

The WebSocket connection stays open for the duration of a game session. The status site connects to this same server using a special observer identity.

**Client to Server messages**:

| Message Type | When Sent | Payload |
|---|---|---|
| JOIN | On connection | userId, username |
| PLAYER_UPDATE | 10 times per second (game clients only) | userId, x, y, z, health |
| ADMIN_SET_TIME | When admin changes time scale | timeScale, paused |

**Server to Client messages**:

| Message Type | When Sent | Payload |
|---|---|---|
| WORLD_SNAPSHOT | 10 times per second to all clients | simTime, timeScale, paused, epoch, bootstrapPhase, bootstrapProgress, all player positions, all NPC positions |
| PLAYER_JOINED | When a new player connects | player data |
| PLAYER_LEFT | When a player disconnects | userId |

### Clerk (Authentication)
Clerk provides the sign-in UI, manages sessions, and issues JWTs. The frontend uses useAuth() and useUser() hooks. The backend uses verifyToken() to validate JWTs server-side on every protected request. The status site does not use Clerk and requires no login.

### Neon Postgres (Database)
Serverless Postgres accessed via the @neondatabase/serverless driver, which is optimized for short-lived serverless function connections. SQL queries are written as tagged template literals, which automatically sanitize inputs and prevent SQL injection.

---

## 12. Data and Database

### Tables

**player_saves** — one row per player

| Column | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| user_id | TEXT (primary key) | Clerk user ID |
| username | TEXT | Display name |
| pos_x, pos_y, pos_z | REAL | Last known world position |
| health, hunger, thirst, energy, fatigue | REAL | Vitals (0 to 1) |
| ev_points | INTEGER | Accumulated evolution points |
| civ_tier | INTEGER | Current civilization tier |
| discoveries | TEXT | JSON array of discovery IDs (stored as text) |
| current_goal | TEXT | Active high-level goal |
| sim_seconds | REAL | Cumulative simulation time experienced |
| updated_at | TIMESTAMPTZ | Last save timestamp |

**world_settings** — a single row representing the shared world state

| Column | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| id | Integer | Always 1 |
| time_scale | REAL | Current simulation speed multiplier |
| sim_time | REAL | Authoritative simulation clock time in seconds |
| updated_at | TIMESTAMPTZ | Last write timestamp |

The Railway server reads world_settings at startup to restore the clock to where it was before any restart. It saves the current sim_time and time_scale to the database every 30 seconds.

### Data Concerns
- discoveries is stored as a serialized JSON string in a TEXT column rather than a native JSONB column. This means the database cannot query or index individual discoveries.
- No database migration system is in place. Schema changes require manually running init-db or writing raw SQL against the Neon console.
- The world_settings table schema is managed by WorldSettingsSync.js with a migrateSchema() call at server startup.
- The simulation grid (chemistry cells) is client-side only — it is not persisted to the database. This means active fires are lost when the player disconnects. Persistence of grid state is a future consideration.

---

## 13. Deployment and Infrastructure

### Frontend (Vercel)

The React application is built with Vite and deployed to Vercel. The build process first type-checks the entire project, then bundles it for the browser. The Vite config defines two named entry points: `main` (the game at `index.html`) and `status` (the dashboard at `status/index.html`). Both are compiled in a single build pass and deployed together.

**Required browser headers** configured in vercel.json:
- Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy: same-origin — required for SharedArrayBuffer, which WebAssembly needs
- Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy: require-corp — required alongside the above

These two headers are essential. Without them, the Rapier3D physics engine would fail to initialize silently. They also enable SharedArrayBuffer, which the chemistry and physics workers use for lock-free grid state.

### API Layer (Vercel Serverless Functions)

The api/ directory is automatically deployed as serverless functions by Vercel. Each TypeScript file becomes an HTTP endpoint. They run on Node.js with auto-parsed JSON request bodies.

### Game Server (Railway)

A dedicated always-on Node.js process running on Railway. It:
- Starts at boot, loads settings from the database, and begins ticking the world clock
- Accepts WebSocket connections on a configurable port (default 8080)
- Broadcasts world state 10 times per second regardless of player count
- Persists sim time and time scale to the database every 30 seconds
- Restarts automatically on crash via Railway's process supervision

### Environment Variables Required

| Variable | Where Used | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| DATABASE_URL | API routes + Railway server | Neon Postgres connection string |
| CLERK_SECRET_KEY | API routes | Verifies Clerk JWTs server-side |
| ADMIN_USER_ID | API routes | The Clerk user ID of the admin account |
| VITE_ADMIN_USER_ID | Frontend build | Shows or hides the admin button (client-side convenience only) |
| VITE_WS_URL | Frontend build | WebSocket URL for the Railway server — also used by the status site |

---

## 14. Status Website — Mission Control Dashboard

### What It Is

The status website is a live, public-facing dashboard for the Universe Simulation game world. It is a completely separate web page, accessible at `/status/` on the deployed Vercel site, that anyone can open in a browser — no login, no account, no game client required.

Think of it as "Mission Control": a read-only window into the game server that shows where players are, what the NPCs are doing, what era of cosmic time the simulation is in, and whether the server is running. It updates live, ten times per second, pulling data from the same Railway WebSocket server that powers the game itself.

### How to Access It

Navigate to `https://universe-sim-beryl.vercel.app/status/` in any browser. The page loads instantly and immediately begins connecting to the Railway server. No credentials are required.

### How It Works Technically

**Build and deployment.** The status site is compiled as part of the standard Vite build using a second named entry point (`status: status/index.html`) defined in `vite.config.ts`. It is deployed automatically alongside the main game on every Vercel deployment.

**Entry point.** `status/index.html` loads the IBM Plex Mono and Orbitron fonts from Google Fonts, sets a black background, and mounts the React application from `src/status/main.tsx`.

**WebSocket connection (`src/status/hooks/useStatusSocket.ts`).** This is the data engine of the entire dashboard. Here is exactly what it does:

1. On mount, it opens a WebSocket connection to the Railway server using the same `VITE_WS_URL` environment variable that the game uses.
2. It immediately sends a `JOIN` message identifying itself as `userId: "__status_viewer__"` and `username: "~observer"`. This causes the server to include it in its broadcast list, so it receives all `WORLD_SNAPSHOT` messages.
3. Every time a `WORLD_SNAPSHOT` arrives (up to 10 times per second), it parses the JSON and updates a React state object called `WorldStatus`.
4. If the connection drops, it automatically reconnects using exponential backoff — starting at 1 second, doubling each attempt, capping at 30 seconds.
5. The observer's own entry is filtered out of the player list, so "~observer" never appears as a player on the dashboard.
6. If `VITE_WS_URL` is not set (for example in a local dev environment without configuration), the hook logs a warning and shows the dashboard in an "offline" state rather than throwing an error.

**Data received from the server.** Each `WORLD_SNAPSHOT` message contains:

| Field | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| epoch | string | Current cosmological era (e.g., "stellar") |
| simTime | number | Universe age in seconds of simulation time |
| timeScale | number | How many simulation-seconds pass per real second |
| paused | boolean | Whether an admin has paused the simulation |
| bootstrapPhase | boolean | Whether the world is still initializing after a server restart |
| bootstrapProgress | number | Initialization progress from 0 to 1 |
| players | array | Each connected player: userId, username, x, y, z, health (0–1) |
| npcs | array | Each NPC: id, x, y, z, state (wander/gather/eat/rest/socialize), hunger |

**What is available from the server vs. what requires being in-game.** This distinction is important:

| Data | Available on Status Site | Only Available In-Game |
|---|---|---|
| Player position (x, y, z) | Yes | |
| Player health (0–1 fraction) | Yes | |
| Player username | Yes | |
| Satiety (hunger level) | | Yes |
| Hydration (thirst level) | | Yes |
| Energy level | | Yes |
| Stamina level | | Yes |
| Inventory contents | | Yes |
| Evolution points | | Yes |
| Current goal | | Yes |
| Civilization tier | | Yes |
| Ambient temperature | | Yes |
| NPC position | Yes | |
| NPC behavior state | Yes | |
| NPC hunger level | Yes | |
| World epoch | Yes | |
| Simulation time | Yes | |
| Time scale multiplier | Yes | |
| Paused state | Yes | |
| Bootstrap progress | Yes | |

The `PlayerDetail` component's source code includes a comment that directly acknowledges this limit: *"Full vitals visible in-game only. Server telemetry: position + health."*

### Layout of the Dashboard

The dashboard is a full-screen, four-zone layout with a space aesthetic (dark background, animated starfield, cyan accent colors, monospace fonts throughout):

```
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|  UNIVERSE SIM  MISSION CONTROL  |  STELLAR ERA  |  SYNC  |  <- EpochBar
+------------------------------------------+---------------+
|                                          |               |
|                                          |  SERVER       |
|           SATELLITE MAP                  |  STATUS       |
|         (canvas, live positions)         |               |
|                                          |  SIDEBAR      |
|                                          |               |
+------------------------------------------+---------------+
|  ACTIVE AGENTS  [count]                                  |  <- PlayerRoster
|  [card] [card] [card] ...                                |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
```

A player detail modal slides up from the bottom-left when a player is clicked.

### Component Details

---

#### EpochBar (`src/status/components/EpochBar.tsx`)

The top strip spanning the full width of the dashboard. It contains three zones:

**Left zone** — The application identity: "UNIVERSE SIM" in the Orbitron font (a futuristic typeface) followed by "MISSION CONTROL" in small uppercase letters.

**Center zone** — The current cosmological epoch name, displayed in large uppercase letters. The component maps internal epoch identifiers (e.g., `stellar`, `dark_ages`, `nucleosynthesis`) to human-readable labels for all 14 defined eras:

| Internal ID | Display Name |
|---|---|
| planck | Planck Epoch |
| grand_unification | Grand Unification |
| electroweak | Electroweak Era |
| quark_epoch | Quark Epoch |
| nucleosynthesis | Nucleosynthesis |
| photon_epoch | Photon Epoch |
| dark_ages | Cosmic Dark Ages |
| reionization | Reionization |
| stellar | Stellar Era |
| galactic | Galactic Era |
| contemporary | Contemporary Era |
| stellar_late | Late Stellar Era |
| degenerate | Degenerate Era |
| dark_era | Dark Era |

When the epoch changes, the text briefly flashes gold for 2 seconds before returning to white. This gives a visible signal to observers that the universe has crossed into a new era.

**Right zone** — Three inline status readouts:
- "WORLD TIME" — the simulation age formatted intelligently depending on scale: shows seconds for very young universes, then minutes, years, kiloyears (kyr), megayears (Myr), and gigayears (Gyr) as the sim time grows
- "TIME SCALE" — the current speed multiplier (e.g., "1×", "1,000×", "1.00e+6×")
- A PAUSED / LIVE badge with a glowing dot — green when running, red when paused
- A SYNC / OFFLINE indicator dot showing whether the WebSocket is currently connected

---

#### SatelliteMap (`src/status/components/SatelliteMap.tsx`)

The main panel, occupying most of the screen. It is a canvas (an HTML drawing surface, 800×600 internal resolution, stretched to fill the available space) that renders entirely through JavaScript drawing commands — no 3D engine, no image assets.

**Terrain generation.** When the component first loads, it generates a procedural terrain texture using fractional Brownian motion (fBm) — a technique that stacks multiple layers of smooth random noise at different scales to produce natural-looking landforms. The result is a convincing top-down landscape with deep ocean (dark blue), shallow water (lighter blue), sandy shorelines (tan), grassland (green), forest and highland (darker green), rocky ridges (gray), and snow-capped peaks (white/gray). This terrain is generated once and cached; it does not change while the page is open.

**Live entity rendering.** On top of the static terrain, the canvas redraws 60 times per second (using `requestAnimationFrame`) to show live entity positions:

- **NPC dots** — Small circles (2.2px radius) color-coded by their current behavior state:
  - Green: wandering
  - Amber/orange: gathering resources
  - Bright green: eating
  - Blue: resting
  - Purple: socializing

- **Player dots** — A more prominent marker for each connected player:
  - A bright green filled circle (5px core)
  - Two concentric rings that pulse in and out over time (the pulse timing is offset per player so they don't all pulse together)
  - A color-coded health arc drawn around the outside of the rings: green above 50% health, orange between 25–50%, red below 25%
  - The player's username label above their dot

**Grid and compass.** A faint cyan coordinate grid is drawn over the terrain, with axis labels at 50-unit intervals. N/S/E/W compass labels appear at the edges. A central crosshair marks the origin (0, 0). Corner bracket decorations (like camera viewfinder corners) complete the "tactical display" aesthetic. A blinking red "LIVE" indicator pulses in the top-right corner of the map.

**World scale.** The map covers a ±220 unit radius around the world origin, matching the NPC wander boundary configured in the server's NpcManager.

**Clicking a player.** Clicking anywhere on the map finds the nearest player dot within a 20-pixel threshold. If a player is found, their detail card slides up (handled by the PlayerDetail component).

**Legend.** A small color legend in the bottom-left corner identifies each dot color.

---

#### ServerStats (`src/status/components/ServerStats.tsx`)

The right-side sidebar, 240px wide, scrollable. It shows:

- **Connection status** — A green (connected) or red (disconnected) dot with text
- **Players Online** — The count of connected players, shown in large cyan digits
- **NPCs Active** — The count of active NPCs
- **NPC Behaviour breakdown** — When NPCs are present, a mini bar chart shows the proportion of NPCs in each behavioral state. Each state gets a color-coded progress bar that animates smoothly as the proportions shift. States are sorted by count (most common first).
- **World Forming progress bar** — Shown only when `bootstrapPhase` is true (i.e., the server has just restarted and is reinitializing the world). A gold progress bar fills from 0 to 100% as initialization completes.
- **Epoch description** — A one-sentence plain-English description of the current cosmological era, explaining what is happening in the universe at this point in time. All 14 eras have descriptions. Examples:
  - Stellar Era: "Galaxies and star systems form across the cosmos."
  - Dark Ages: "Hydrogen fills the void but no stars shine yet."
  - Degenerate Era: "Only white dwarfs, neutron stars and black holes remain."
- **Footer** — "UNIVERSE SIM v0.1 / STATUS OBSERVER / 10 Hz TELEMETRY"

---

#### PlayerRoster (`src/status/components/PlayerRoster.tsx`)

The bottom strip, 90px tall, containing horizontally scrollable player cards. Each card shows:

- An avatar square displaying the player's initials (first letter of each word in the username) in the Orbitron font
- The player's full username
- A health bar (green above 60%, orange 30–60%, red below 30%) with a percentage label
- The player's X and Z coordinates (map position, in world units, rounded to the nearest integer)

Cards are clickable — clicking one opens the PlayerDetail modal for that player and highlights the card with a green glow. Clicking the same card again deselects it.

When no players are online, the strip shows: "NO AGENTS ONLINE — AWAITING CONNECTION".

---

#### PlayerDetail (`src/status/components/PlayerDetail.tsx`)

A modal card that slides up from the bottom-left corner of the screen when a player is selected (either by clicking their roster card or by clicking their dot on the satellite map). Clicking anywhere outside the card dismisses it.

The card contains:
- A header with the player's initials avatar, their username, and a truncated Agent ID (first 12 characters of their userId)
- A Vitals section showing health as a progress bar with an icon and percentage — the bar turns red and glows when below 25%
- A Coordinates section showing X, Y, and Z position values in a three-column grid, each to one decimal place
- A note at the bottom: "Full vitals visible in-game only. Server telemetry: position + health."

The modal enters and exits with a 250ms fade and slide-up animation.

### Design Aesthetic

The status site has a deliberate "military mission control" look. The visual language is cohesive and distinct from generic dashboards:

- Background: near-black (#060810) with an animated, slowly scrolling starfield
- A blue atmospheric glow gradient at the top and bottom edges
- All borders use a faint cyan with 13% opacity, giving panels a subtle separation without hard lines
- Primary accent color: cyan (#00d4ff)
- Alert/warning: orange (#ff6b35)
- Health/live: green (#00ff88)
- Gold (#ffd700) used for epoch transitions and world-forming state
- Typography: IBM Plex Mono for all data readouts (monospace gives the readout-screen feel), Orbitron for headings and player initials (geometric/futuristic)
- Custom scrollbar styling (4px, cyan-tinted)

The overall effect is a convincing "operations center" display consistent with the cosmological theme of the game.

---

## 15. What Was Fixed and Built — All Sessions

### Prior Sessions (2026-03-19) — Summary

Fixes 1–10 were applied in previous sessions. They are documented in full in Report Version 3. Brief summary:

- Fix 1: Vercel API routes rewritten to Node.js handler style (save/load/world-settings were crashing with 500 errors)
- Fix 2: Admin time-scale requests now include the Clerk JWT (were silently returning 401)
- Fix 3: Mouse cursor now released when any panel opens (was staying locked, blocking panel interaction)
- Fix 4: Dead event listeners removed from SceneRoot
- Fix 5: Click-to-play overlay added (game was unenterable without it)
- Fix 6: Camera distance increased from 6m to 8m
- Fix 7: Admin WebSocket variable correctly assigned (admin commands were being dropped)
- Fix 8: Rapier3D physics engine fully integrated — planet trimesh collider, player capsule, character controller, gravity toward planet center
- Fix 9: Terrain triangle winding fixed — player no longer falls through the floor
- Fix 10: DevUser ghost copy removed — player's own data is no longer stored as a remote entity

---

### Session: Simulation Grid Phase 1 — 2026-03-20

This session delivered the first real chemistry simulation in the game. Below is a technical summary of each of the 10 components built.

---

**Component 1: MaterialRegistry**

`src/sim/MaterialRegistry.ts` — A lookup table mapping 11 material IDs to their thermal and combustion constants:

| Material | k (W/m·K) | Cp (J/kg·K) | density (kg/m³) | ignitionTemp (°C) | combustionJ_kg (MJ/kg) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| air | 0.026 | 1005 | 1.2 | — | 0 |
| stone | 2.0 | 840 | 2500 | — | 0 |
| flint | 1.5 | 800 | 2600 | — | 0 |
| wood | 0.17 | 1700 | 600 | 300 | 18 |
| bark | 0.15 | 1600 | 500 | 250 | 17 |
| fiber | 0.05 | 1400 | 200 | 230 | 16 |
| clay | 1.0 | 920 | 1800 | — | 0 |
| coal | 0.26 | 1000 | 1350 | 400 | 29 |
| iron | 80 | 450 | 7874 | — | 0 |
| copper | 400 | 385 | 8960 | — | 0 |

These are real-world values. k is thermal conductivity (how fast heat moves through the material). Cp is specific heat capacity (how much energy it takes to heat 1 kg by 1°C). ignitionTemp is the temperature at which combustion starts. combustionJ_kg is energy released per kg burned.

---

**Component 2: GridCoords**

`src/sim/GridCoords.ts` — Five pure utility functions for working with the simulation grid:

- `worldToGrid(pos, origin, cellSize)` — converts a world position (metres) to an integer grid index
- `gridToWorldCenter(idx, origin, cellSize)` — converts a grid index back to the center of that cell in world space
- `isInBounds(idx, dims)` — returns true if an index is inside the grid dimensions
- `initO2Moles(cellSize, tempC)` — calculates the initial oxygen content of an air cell using the ideal gas law: n = PV/RT where P = 21% of atmospheric pressure (oxygen fraction of air), V = cell volume, R = 8.314 J/mol·K, T = temperature in Kelvin
- `computeGridOrigin(spawnPos, dims, cellSize)` — centers the grid on the spawn point

---

**Component 3: Arrhenius**

`src/sim/Arrhenius.ts` — Pure combustion math:

- `arrheniusRate(A, Ea, tempC)` — implements k = A × exp(-Ea / RT). A is the pre-exponential factor, Ea is activation energy in J/mol, R is 8.314 J/mol·K, T is temperature in Kelvin. Returns the reaction rate constant.
- `combustionStep(cell, dt)` — runs one time step of combustion for a single grid cell. If the cell material is flammable (wood, bark, fiber, or coal) and temperature exceeds the ignition point, it calculates how much mass burns in dt seconds using Arrhenius kinetics, releases that energy as heat, consumes proportional O2 from the cell, reduces cell density by the burned mass, and returns updated cell state.

---

**Component 4: Thermal Worker Fix**

`src/workers/thermalWorker.ts` — Previously used hardcoded water values (k = 0.6 W/m·K, Cp = 4182 J/kg·K) for all materials regardless of what material was actually in each cell. Now uses per-material inline lookup tables derived from MaterialRegistry. This means stone cells now conduct heat like stone (much faster than water), wood cells insulate like wood, and so on.

---

**Component 5: Chemistry Worker**

`src/workers/chemWorker.ts` — A new Web Worker that runs at 10 Hz:

- Receives TICK messages to run one combustion step across all grid cells
- Receives IGNITE messages to deposit a configurable energy amount into a specific cell (used when the player strikes flint near wood)
- Receives PLACE_MATERIAL messages to set a cell's material (used when the player places a wood object in the world)
- Runs burnout detection each tick — if a cell's density drops below 1 kg/m³, it converts to air (the material has fully combusted)
- Tracks O2 consumption per tick — burning cells reduce local oxygen, which can eventually extinguish fire if the space is sealed

---

**Component 6: LocalSimManager and SimulationEngine**

`src/engine/LocalSimManager.ts` — Bridges the player's game world to the chemistry grid:

- `initFromSpawn(spawnPos, terrain)` — fills the grid based on terrain: stone cells below the surface, air+O2 cells above
- `placeWood(pos)` — deposits a wood cell at a world position (sends PLACE_MATERIAL to chemWorker)
- `ignite(pos, joules)` — deposits heat energy into a cell (sends IGNITE to chemWorker)
- `getTemperatureAt(pos)` — returns the current temperature of the grid cell at a world position
- `getHotCells()` — returns all cells currently above 200°C for the fire renderer

`src/engine/SimulationEngine.ts` — Gained a `gridOrigin` field (the world position of the grid's corner) and a `sendToChem(msg)` method for forwarding messages to the chemistry worker.

---

**Component 7: playerStore**

`src/store/playerStore.ts` — Added two new fields:
- `ambientTemp: number` — defaults to 15°C, represents the temperature near the player's current position
- `setAmbientTemp(t: number)` — action to update the ambient temperature each frame

---

**Component 8: SceneRoot**

`src/rendering/SceneRoot.tsx` — Wired LocalSimManager into the game loop:

- LocalSimManager is created once after the engine initialises
- Each frame, SceneRoot calls `localSim.getTemperatureAt(playerPos)` and calls `setAmbientTemp` to update the HUD
- The fire-start interaction is triggered when: flint is equipped AND the player presses the attack key AND a wood or bark resource node is within 3 metres. This calls `localSim.placeWood(nodePos)` then `localSim.ignite(nodePos, 50000)` (50 kJ — enough to start combustion)
- The "Fire started!" notification is shown via the toast system

---

**Component 9: FireRenderer**

`src/rendering/FireRenderer.tsx` — A React Three Fiber component:

- Polls `localSim.getHotCells()` every 100ms (10 Hz) using a delta accumulator in `useFrame`
- For each hot cell, renders a `<mesh>` with a `<sphereGeometry>` (radius scales with temperature from 0.1m at 200°C to 0.4m at 1000°C+) and a glowing orange `<meshStandardMaterial>` with emissive color
- Also renders a `<pointLight>` per hot cell with intensity proportional to temperature and a 4-metre influence radius

---

**Component 10: HUD**

`src/ui/HUD.tsx` — Now reads `ambientTemp` from playerStore and displays it below the vitals bars:

- Shows "AMBIENT: XX.X°C"
- Color coding: blue below 0, green 0–30, orange 30–50, red 50+
- Updates every frame as the player moves

---

### Session: Status Website — 2026-03-20

**What was built**: A complete, standalone Mission Control dashboard at `/status/` covering 9 source files across the `src/status/` subtree and a new `status/index.html` entry point. The Vite build config was updated to define the second entry point. Full details are documented in Section 14 of this report.

---

### Test Coverage Added — Prior Session

18 new Vitest unit tests, all passing:

**MaterialRegistry (4 tests)**
- Returns correct k for wood
- Returns correct ignitionTemp for coal
- Returns zero combustionJ_kg for stone
- Returns correct density for iron

**GridCoords (8 tests)**
- worldToGrid returns [0,0,0] for spawn at origin
- worldToGrid returns correct positive index for positive offset
- gridToWorldCenter returns cell center at half-cellSize from origin
- isInBounds returns true for center cell
- isInBounds returns false for negative index
- isInBounds returns false for index at grid edge
- initO2Moles returns positive value at 20°C
- computeGridOrigin centers grid on spawn position

**Arrhenius (6 tests)**
- arrheniusRate returns zero at room temperature for wood (below ignition)
- arrheniusRate returns positive value above ignition temperature
- combustionStep returns unchanged cell below ignition temperature
- combustionStep reduces density above ignition temperature
- combustionStep increases cell temperature above ignition temperature
- combustionStep consumes O2 proportionally to mass burned

---

## 16. Known Issues and Gaps

### Active Bugs

| ID | Issue | Severity | Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| B-01 | admin.ts still uses Web API style (Request/Response) and will return 500 errors in production | Medium | The admin player list table will fail to load. The admin panel opens but shows no players. |
| B-02 | No toast notification when a save fails | Low | Players have no feedback if auto-save silently fails due to a database or network error |
| B-03 | @clerk/clerk-react and @clerk/react are both listed as dependencies at different versions | Low | May cause subtle import conflicts or increase bundle size unnecessarily |
| B-04 | Simulation grid state is not persisted | Low | Active fires are lost when the player disconnects or refreshes the page |
| B-05 | Status site canvas is fixed at 800x600 internal resolution and stretches to fill the container | Low | On very wide or very tall viewports, players and NPCs near the edges may appear slightly offset from where they are clicked. The click-detection math accounts for this scaling but the visual alignment may look imprecise at extreme aspect ratios. |

### Missing Gameplay Features

| Feature | Why It Matters |
|---|---|
| Fire spread | Fire currently burns in the cell where it starts but does not spread to adjacent combustible cells. True propagation requires checking neighbours each tick. |
| NPC interaction | NPCs wander but clicking or approaching them does nothing. They are visual placeholders only. |
| Terrain height variation | The world is a flat plane with no hills, valleys, rivers, or landmarks. Navigation has no meaningful geography. |
| Tech Tree content | Panel opens but shows nothing |
| Evolution panel content | Panel opens but shows nothing |
| Journal content | Panel opens but shows nothing |
| Character panel content | Panel opens but shows nothing |
| Map panel content | Panel opens but shows nothing. A minimap would be especially useful given the featureless terrain. |
| Settings panel content | Panel opens but shows nothing |
| Player death and respawn | Vitals deplete over time but what happens when they reach zero is not implemented |
| Mobile and touch support | The game requires a mouse and keyboard — not playable on phones or tablets |
| Save-failed notification | The error path in saveGame() has no user-facing feedback |
| Cooking | Food placed near fire should change properties — this requires reading grid temperature in the crafting system |

---

## 17. Recommendations

Recommendations are listed in priority order. Each explains the player impact.

### Priority 1 — Extend the Fire System (Highest Gameplay Value)

**Implement fire spread.**
The chemistry worker already processes every cell each tick. Adding spread requires: for each burning cell, check the six adjacent cells; if an adjacent cell is combustible (wood, bark, fiber, coal) and its temperature exceeds its ignition point, begin burning it. This is a small change that turns a static burning point into a realistic spreading fire — one of the most viscerally satisfying things in any survival game.

**Add cooking.**
The grid already tracks temperature at every cell. The crafting system only needs to check whether a food item is inside a hot cell before allowing cooking recipes. This wires the chemistry simulation directly into the progression system for the first time.

### Priority 2 — Fix Known Bugs (Small Changes, High Impact)

**Fix admin.ts to use Node.js handler style.**
The admin player table will return 500 errors because admin.ts was not updated in the fix session. This is a small change matching the pattern already applied to save.ts, load.ts, and world-settings.ts.

**Add a save-failed toast notification.**
saveStore.ts currently ignores errors from the save endpoint. If the Neon database is unreachable, players lose progress with no warning.

### Priority 3 — Player Experience (Near-term)

**Add terrain height variation.**
Even a simple procedurally generated heightmap applied to the existing terrain mesh would dramatically improve the sense of being in a real world. The terrain geometry already has 63x63 subdivisions — applying a noise function to vertex Y-positions is a modest change with high visual impact. The status site's satellite map would automatically reflect any terrain changes since it generates its own procedural texture.

**Resolve the duplicate Clerk package.**
Remove either @clerk/clerk-react or @clerk/react from package.json and standardize all imports on one.

**Add a zoom control to the satellite map.**
The status site currently shows a fixed ±220 unit radius. As the game world grows, a scroll-wheel zoom would let observers inspect player positions more precisely.

### Priority 4 — Robustness (Medium-term)

**Authenticate ADMIN_SET_TIME on the server.**
The Railway WebSocket server currently accepts ADMIN_SET_TIME messages from any connected client without verifying identity.

**Consider adding optional authentication to the status WebSocket feed.**
Currently any person who finds the status URL can observe player positions and health in real time. This may be intentional (public transparency), but if player privacy becomes a concern, the server could require a token before sending player data to observer connections.

**Expand test coverage to inventory, crafting, and player systems.**
Vitest is configured and proven. The MetabolismSystem drain rates, the canCraft() recipe filter, and the saveGame serialization are good next targets.

**Add rate limiting to save and load endpoints.**

**Persist simulation grid state to the database.**
Active fires are lost on disconnect. Storing the grid as a compressed binary blob in a new table would let the world remember its state correctly.

### Priority 5 — Content (Ongoing)

Populate the six empty panel placeholders in this suggested order:
1. Character panel — show player stats, civ tier, evolution points in a formatted view
2. Map panel — a top-down minimap showing the player's position and surroundings
3. Tech Tree panel — visualize unlockable technologies and their requirements
4. Evolution panel — show available evolution upgrades purchased with accumulated EP
5. Journal panel — log discoveries and world events as they happen
6. Settings panel — graphics quality, audio volume, keybinding configuration

---

## 18. Summary Scorecard

| Area | Rating | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Core Architecture | Excellent | Clean separation of frontend, API, and game server. WebSocket design is solid. Workers isolated from render loop. Status site cleanly isolated in its own subtree. |
| Multiplayer | Good | Real-time sync works. NPCs synchronized from server. Auto-reconnect handled correctly. |
| Authentication | Excellent | Clerk integration is correct. JWT verification on every protected endpoint. Status site intentionally public. |
| Save and Load | Good | Working after prior fixes. No error handling or user notification on failure. Grid state not persisted. |
| Admin Controls | Good | Working after prior fixes. WebSocket command auth is missing on the server side. |
| Physics | Good | Rapier3D fully integrated. Planet trimesh collider active. Player capsule + character controller working. Gravity toward planet center. |
| Chemistry Simulation | Good | Phase 1 complete. Arrhenius combustion, per-material heat, O2 consumption, fire rendering all working. Fire spread not yet implemented. |
| Gameplay Loop | Improved | Gather + equip + craft + fire-start chain is end-to-end. Fire spread, cooking, and NPC interaction still missing. |
| Status Website | Good | Live dashboard fully functional. All five components working. Procedural terrain, pulsing player dots, NPC state breakdown, epoch descriptions, player detail modal all operational. |
| User Interface and Design | Good | Consistent aesthetic. Panel system complete and functional. Six panels have no content. Status site adds a second cohesive design language. |
| Performance | Good | Efficient rendering for current complexity. Chemistry runs in a worker thread. Status site canvas redraws at 60 fps but has no heavy computation. |
| Accessibility | Needs Improvement | Buttons labeled correctly. No keyboard navigation into panels. No focus trapping. Status site also lacks keyboard accessibility. |
| Code Quality | Good | Well-organized and readable. Pure simulation functions are exemplary. admin.ts inconsistency persists. Status site code is clean and self-contained. |
| Security | Good | Auth is correct. One unauthenticated WebSocket command is a known medium-severity risk. Status site exposes position + health publicly — low severity, intentional design. |
| Database Design | Good | Schema is appropriate. discoveries stored as TEXT. Grid state not yet persisted. |
| Test Coverage | Partial | 18 tests covering simulation math modules. All other game logic remains untested. Status site components have no tests. |
| Content Completeness | Needs Improvement | Six of eight panels are empty placeholders. Fire spread and cooking not yet built. |

**Overall Health: Mission Control Now Online**

The infrastructure is solid and all blocking server errors have been resolved. The Rapier3D physics engine provides correct character movement. Simulation Grid Phase 1 delivers the first genuinely science-grounded gameplay: a real Arrhenius combustion reaction that consumes oxygen, generates heat, renders visible fire, and warms the player based on proximity. The full gather-to-fire chain works end-to-end. The new Status Website adds a permanent public window into the game world — anyone can watch the simulation live without logging in. With fire spread and cooking added, the early-game survival loop will be complete enough to be genuinely engaging.
